section.title: Sarah's Burial
section.sectionRationale: Sarah dies, and Abraham buys a field and a cave to bury her. It reads like a legal deed, yet it is quietly momentous: the sojourner who was promised the whole land now owns his first small plot of it, a burial ground held in hope of resurrection.
section.thesis: In grief Abraham insists on paying full price for a burial site, and the man who owns nothing gains his first foothold in the promised land, a down payment of the inheritance held by faith beyond death.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: Sarah's death and mourning; Abraham's request for a burial site; the careful purchase of Machpelah; the field secured as his possession.

verses.text: Now Sarah lived to be 127 years old.
She died in Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron) in the land of Canaan, and Abraham went out to mourn and to weep for her.

units.label: Abraham asks for a burial site

verses.text: Then Abraham got up from beside his dead wife and said to the Hittites,
“I am a {{ger|foreigner and an outsider}} among you. Give me a burial site among you so that I can bury my dead.”
The Hittites replied to Abraham,
“Listen to us, sir. You are God’s chosen one among us. Bury your dead in the finest of our tombs. None of us will withhold his tomb for burying your dead.”
Then Abraham rose and bowed down before the people of the land, the Hittites.
“If you are willing for me to bury my dead,” he said to them, “listen to me, and approach Ephron son of Zohar on my behalf
to sell me the {{machpelah|cave of Machpelah}} that belongs to him; it is at the end of his field. Let him sell it to me in your presence for full price, so that I may have a burial site.”
Now Ephron was sitting among the sons of Heth. So in the presence of all the Hittites who had come to the gate of his city, Ephron the Hittite answered Abraham,
“No, my lord. Listen to me. I give you the field, and I give you the cave that is in it. I give it to you in the presence of my people. Bury your dead.”
Again Abraham bowed down before the people of the land
and said to Ephron in their presence, “If you will please listen to me, I will pay you the price of the field. Accept it from me, so that I may bury my dead there.”
Ephron answered Abraham,
“Listen to me, my lord. The land is worth four hundred shekels of silver, but what is that between you and me? Bury your dead.”
Abraham agreed to Ephron’s terms and weighed out for him the price he had named in the hearing of the Hittites: four hundred shekels of silver, according to the standard of the merchants.

units.label: The field secured as a possession
units.note: a foothold in the promised land

verses.text: So Ephron’s field at Machpelah near Mamre, the cave that was in it, and all the trees within the boundaries of the field were deeded over
to Abraham’s {{achuzzah|possession}} in the presence of all the Hittites who had come to the gate of his city.
After this, Abraham buried his wife Sarah in the cave of the field at Machpelah near Mamre (that is, Hebron) in the land of Canaan.
So the field and its cave were deeded by the Hittites to Abraham as a burial site.

ger.gloss: A resident foreigner, a sojourner who lives among a people without full rights of citizenship or landholding. Abraham calls himself a ger to explain why he must ask to buy ground. The word captures the whole patriarchal life, dwelling in the land of promise as strangers who own none of it yet.

machpelah.gloss: A place name for the field and cave near Hebron that Abraham buys, likely meaning double or the doubled cave. It becomes the ancestral tomb of the patriarchs, where Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah are buried, Israel's first and abiding claim on the land.

achuzzah.gloss: A holding, property, or possession, from a root meaning to grasp or take hold. It is the formal legal term for owned land. That the promised land first becomes Abraham's achuzzah as a grave is deeply poignant, a foothold of inheritance secured even in the face of death.

basic.context: Sarah, Abraham's wife and the mother of the promised son, dies at 127. Abraham mourns, then negotiates carefully with the local Hittites to buy a cave and field as a burial place. He refuses to take it as a gift and pays full price. The key question is why the writer devotes a whole chapter to a real estate transaction.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a historical account, and its slow, legal detail is the point. Abraham, promised the whole land, secures his first tiny plot of it, and he does so as a lawful, honoured purchase before witnesses. The question is what this small deed says about faith in God's promise in the face of death.
basic.originalLanguages: Abraham calls himself a ger, a resident foreigner with no land rights, which is why he must buy ground. He purchases the cave of Machpelah, and it becomes his achuzzah, his legal possession or holding. That the promised land is first held as a grave gives the ordinary words a weight of hope.
basic.typology: The burial ground held in a land not yet possessed is a picture of the whole life of faith, which holds God's promises even through death. The cave where the dead are laid in hope anticipates the tomb that could not hold Christ, and the resurrection life His people await.
basic.christ: Abraham buries Sarah trusting God beyond the grave, the same hope fulfilled when Christ rose from a borrowed tomb. The down payment of the land points to the inheritance secured for believers, and to the day the whole earth is given to the meek in the risen Lord.
basic.god: God is faithful to His promise even when its fulfilment seems distant and death intervenes. He gives Abraham a real, if small, foothold in the land, a token that His word will not fail. He is the God of the living, in whom the buried are held in hope.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 11 says the patriarchs died in faith, not having received the promises, but seeing them from afar. Acts 7 recalls this burial plot. Genesis 49 and 50 return to Machpelah as the patriarchs' tomb.
basic.oneStory: The sojourner gains his first plot of the promised land, a grave, held in hope that God keeps His word beyond death. It is a quiet pledge of the full inheritance, secured at last in the risen Christ who opens the tomb.

context.literary: A change of pace after the intensity of chapter 22, told in the measured cadences of a legal transaction, with formal courtesies, public witnesses, and precise sums. Its very ordinariness underlines a theological point: the promised land begins to be possessed, one field at a time.
context.historical: The negotiation reflects ancient Near Eastern property custom: business conducted at the city gate, before witnesses, with elaborate politeness and haggling. Abraham's insistence on full payment secures an unassailable legal title rather than a revocable favour.
context.canonical: Machpelah becomes the ancestral tomb of the patriarchs, Genesis 49:29-32, 50:13, and the first concrete Israelite claim in Canaan. It anchors the promise of the land in a real, deeded place that later generations can point to.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record Sarah's death and honourable burial, and to show Abraham securing lawful, permanent title to a portion of the promised land, a first and hope filled foothold of the inheritance God had sworn.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a burial and a purchase; it does not lay down laws of mourning or commerce. Its abiding lessons are faith that holds God's promise beyond death, honest dealing, and honour shown to the dead.

debates.question: Why does Abraham insist on paying full price when the field is offered to him freely?

views.label: To secure unassailable legal title
views.case: A gift could be reclaimed or disputed; a purchase before witnesses at the city gate gives Abraham a permanent, deeded holding in the land, an achuzzah no one can revoke.

views.label: To act on faith in the promise
views.case: Abraham stakes a real claim in Canaan because he believes God's oath to give him the land; buying rather than borrowing is an act of hope, not mere caution.

debates.bearing: The two readings reinforce each other: legal prudence and faith combine, so that the first parcel of the promised land is held by right and in hope, a genuine down payment on the inheritance.

study.originalLanguages: The language is plain but pointed. Abraham names himself a ger, a resident alien with no standing to own land, which explains the whole negotiation. He buys the field of Machpelah, and it passes to him as his achuzzah, a formal, permanent possession. The irony is deliberate: the land God promised as an everlasting inheritance is first held as a grave, a foothold gained precisely at the point of loss.

typology.type: The burial cave held as a possession in the land
typology.fulfillment: A down payment on the inheritance God promised, pointing to the full inheritance secured for believers in Christ, 1 Peter 1:4.

typology.type: The grave held in hope in the land of promise
typology.fulfillment: The tomb that could not hold Christ, and the resurrection hope of all who die in faith.

study.christ: Abraham lays Sarah in the ground still trusting the God who promised, and buries her in the very land God had sworn to give. That hope in the face of death finds its answer in Christ, who was laid in a borrowed tomb and rose, breaking death's hold. The small deeded field is a token of the inheritance Jesus secures for His people, and a pledge of the day the meek inherit the earth, Matthew 5:5. The patriarchs buried at Machpelah rest in hope of the resurrection Christ has now opened.
study.god: God keeps His word even when its fulfilment is slow and death seems to have the last say. He grants Abraham a real, permanent foothold in the land, a first instalment of the promise. He is the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and, as Jesus says, the God of the living, in whom those who are buried are held safe until the resurrection.

crossReferences.note: The patriarchs died in faith, not having received the promises, but seeing and greeting them from afar as strangers on the earth.
Jacob charges his sons to bury him at Machpelah, the tomb of Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, and Leah.
Stephen recalls the burial ground the patriarchs bought, keeping the memory of this deeded plot alive.
God is the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the God of the living, grounding resurrection hope for the buried patriarchs.

study.oneStory: The man promised a land dies owning nothing but a grave, yet even that grave is a foothold of the inheritance, bought and deeded in hope. It is a small, faith filled claim on God's promise, a claim finally made good in Christ, who rose from the tomb and secures the inheritance for all who die trusting Him.

quiz.q: Why did Abraham need to ask the Hittites for a burial site?

options.text: Because he was a resident foreigner who owned no land in Canaan.
options.note: Abraham calls himself a ger, a sojourner without land rights, in verse 4.

options.text: Because he had not yet settled anywhere in the region.
options.note: He does live in the land, but the real issue is that as a foreigner he owns none of it.

options.text: Because the Hittites had driven him from his own land.
options.note: The Hittites honour him; there is no expulsion in the account.

options.text: Because God had told him not to buy land.
options.note: There is no such command; Abraham freely negotiates to buy the field.

quiz.q: How did Abraham respond to Ephron's offer to give him the field for free?

options.text: He insisted on paying the full price and weighed out the silver.
options.note: Abraham refuses a gift and pays four hundred shekels before witnesses.

options.text: He accepted the field but gave gifts in return.
options.note: He does not accept it as a gift at all; he pays the stated price in full.

options.text: He took the field freely as an honour due to him.
options.note: The opposite; Abraham declines the free offer and insists on buying it.

options.text: He refused the field and buried Sarah elsewhere.
options.note: He buys Machpelah and buries Sarah there.

quiz.q: What is significant about the field of Machpelah becoming Abraham's possession?

options.text: It is his first permanent holding in the promised land, a foothold of the inheritance.
options.note: The land God promised is first held by Abraham as his own deeded achuzzah.

options.text: It shows Abraham had grown wealthy enough to buy land.
options.note: His wealth is real, but the deeper point is the foothold in the promised land.

options.text: It proves the Hittites had surrendered all their territory.
options.note: Abraham buys only one field; the Hittites keep their land.

options.text: It means Abraham had given up hope in God's promise.
options.note: The purchase is an act of hope in the promise, not a giving up of it.

quiz.q: How does this burial account connect to resurrection hope?

options.text: Abraham buries Sarah in the promised land trusting God beyond death, a hope fulfilled in the risen Christ.
options.note: The grave held in hope points to the tomb that could not hold Jesus.

options.text: It teaches that faithful people avoid grief at death.
options.note: Abraham genuinely mourns; the hope does not remove grief but holds through it.

options.text: It shows that the dead simply cease to exist.
options.note: The patriarchs are buried in hope; Jesus calls God the God of the living.

options.text: It proves that the promised land was only ever symbolic.
options.note: The land and the deed are real; they anchor a real inheritance and hope.

quiz.q: What does Abraham's careful, honest purchase reveal about him?

options.text: He deals with integrity before witnesses and stakes a real claim on God's promise.
options.note: Honest dealing and faith in the promise meet in the public, full price purchase.

options.text: He was mainly concerned to appear respectable to the Hittites.
options.note: He is honourable, but the driving motive is a lawful, hope filled claim on the land, not mere appearance.

options.text: He was reluctant to spend money on a burial.
options.note: He willingly pays a large sum; cost is not his hesitation.

options.text: He distrusted God and sought security in property.
options.note: The purchase expresses faith in God's promise, not distrust of it.

questions: Abraham mourned and wept for Sarah, and Scripture does not rush him past it. How does it help to know that honest grief and steady faith can live together?
Abraham held on to God's promise even as he buried the one he loved. What promise of God do you need to hold on to in a season of loss?
The land was first possessed as a grave, a foothold of a much larger inheritance. Where are you being asked to trust God for a fulfilment you can only glimpse now?
Abraham dealt honestly and openly, paying full price before witnesses. Where is God calling you to integrity even when a shortcut is offered?
The buried patriarchs rest in hope of resurrection. How does the empty tomb of Christ change the way you face death, your own and others'?

translationNotes.note: Kiriath-arba is identified as Hebron; the parenthesis preserves both the older name and the later, familiar one.
Four hundred shekels of silver was a substantial sum, weighed out according to the merchants' standard, marking a full and honest price rather than a bargain.
